{{pp-vandalism|small=yes}} {{otheruses|the Ochi clan of Iyo Province|a branch of the Yamato Minamoto clan|Ochi clan (Yamato Province)}} {{Japanese clans (ancient clans) | Name = Ochi clan | Image = | Clan name = Ochi [[Sukune]] | Founder = {{ill|Ochinomikoto|ja|小致命}}
({{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}} lineage, {{ill|Ochi Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|小市国造}}The Ochi clan descends from Mononobe’s Ochinomikoto, who was appointed as the Ochi Kuni no Miyatsuko (Governor of Ochi County) and controlled Ochi District under the title "Atai." In November of 835 ({{ill|Jōwa|ja|承和 (日本)}} 2), his descendant Ochi Hiroshige was granted the surname Sukune from Atai (『{{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}}』). {{Sfn|丹羽|1970|p=355}})
Alternatively, Ochiō
({{ill|Emperor Kōrei|ja|孝霊天皇}}’s third prince, {{ill|Prince Iyo|ja|伊予皇子}}’s third son?) | Origin = | Ancestor = | Type = {{ill|Divine lineage|ja|神別}}
Alternatively {{ill|Imperial lineage|ja|皇別}} | Hometown = {{ill|Iyo Province|ja|伊予国}}, {{ill|Ochi-go|ja|越智郷}} | Figures = | Descendants = {{ill|Kōno clan|ja|河野氏}} (Samurai)
{{ill|Yano clan|ja|矢野氏}} (Samurai)
{{ill|Iyo Tachibana clan|ja|橘氏 (伊予国)}} (Samurai)
{{ill|Mishima family|ja|三島家 (薩摩藩士の家系)}} (Samurai)
{{ill|Nii clan|ja|新居氏}} (Samurai)
{{ill|Imai clan|ja|今井氏#その他の今井氏}} (Samurai) }} '''Ochi clan''' (越智氏) refers to a {{ill|clan|ja|氏族}} with the name "Ochi." The Ochi clan was one of the prominent {{ill|Gōzoku|lt=local ruling clans|ja|豪族}} in ancient {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}, originating in {{ill|Iyo Province|ja|伊予国}} (present-day {{ill|Ehime Prefecture|ja|愛媛県}}).{{cite kotobank|word=越智氏|author=山内 譲|encyclopedia=改訂新版 {{ill|World Encyclopedia|ja|世界大百科事典}}|accessdate=2024-03-10}} Some theories link the clan's origins to its association with the development of the {{ill|Nankaidō|ja|南海道}} region, but this connection remains unverified. == Overview == The Ochi clan is said to have originated in {{ill|Ochi-go|ja|越智郷}} (modern {{ill|Kokubu, Imabari|ja|今治市国分}}). During the late 4th century, the establishment of the {{ill|Kuni no miyatsuko|ja|国造}} system by the Yamato court led to the creation of five Kuni no miyatsuko in western Ehime, including the Ochi clan. The clan’s founder is traditionally considered {{ill|Ochinomikoto|ja|小致命}}, grandson of Ōshinkawa no Mikoto of the {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}} lineage (『{{ill|Kuni no miyatsuko no Hongi|ja|国造本紀}}』). According to the 『{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}』, Ochi no Atai (later Ochi Sukune) was a {{ill|Divine lineage|ja|神別}} clan descending from {{ill|Nigihayahi|ja|饒速日命}}.{{cite web|url=https://www.digital.archives.go.jp/file/1256162.html|title=Shinsen Shōjiroku|website=National Archives of Japan|accessdate=2024-03-10}} During the early 8th century, the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} reorganized the administrative divisions into fourteen districts, designating the former Ochi Kuni no Miyatsuko territory as "Ochi District." The district became the site of Iyo’s {{ill|provincial government|ja|国府}} and {{ill|provincial temple|ja|国分寺}}. Archaeological investigations near Kokubu have suggested a connection between the Ochi clan and the provincial government, but conclusive evidence is lacking. The earliest recorded figure of the Ochi clan is Ochi no Atai, a district governor of Ochi District. According to the 『{{ill|Nihon Ryōiki|ja|日本霊異記}}』, he was captured during the {{ill|Battle of Baekgang|ja|白村江の戦い}} but returned safely thanks to the blessings of {{ill|Kannon|ja|観音菩薩}}, to whom he later dedicated a temple. While the location of this ancient temple remains unknown, it reflects an effort to reassert local authority through Buddhism. In the history of Iyo, the first documented Ochi clan members were Ochi no Atai Asukamaro and Ochi no Atai Kunimaru, who were promoted in 767 ({{ill|Jingō-keiun|ja|神護景雲}} 1) for their tribute offerings. Later, Ochi Mochitada received official recognition in 948 ({{ill|Tenryaku|ja|天暦}} 2) for subduing pirates during the {{ill|Jōhei Tengyō War|ja|承平天慶の乱}}. Despite occasional mentions of the Ochi clan in historical records, the clan disappeared from Iyo’s political scene after Ochi Tamemori’s appointment as a military commissioner in 1002 ({{ill|Chōhō|ja|長保}} 4). == Legacy and Decline == The Ochi clan’s decline is marked by the increasing influence of the {{ill|Kōno clan|ja|河野氏}} in Iyo Province. While sources like 『{{ill|Yoshōki|ja|予章記}}』 provide genealogies linking the Ochi and Kōno clans, these accounts are often embellished with myths, such as tracing the Ochi lineage to Emperor Kōrei’s grandson. Such claims have been met with skepticism due to their lack of historical credibility. According to the {{ill|Ōyamazumi Shrine|ja|大山祇神社}} in modern {{ill|Ōmishima, Imabari|ja|今治市大三島町}}, the Ochi clan became the shrine’s hereditary priests, later adopting the surname {{ill|Mishima|ja|三島家}}. Today, "Ochi" remains a common surname in Imabari City. Notably, during the early Meiji era, documents recorded political leader {{ill|Itō Hirobumi|ja|伊藤博文}} as "Ochi Sukune Hirobumi." == References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist|24em}} == Bibliography == * ''History of Ehime Prefecture – Prefectural History 38'', {{ill|Yamakawa Shuppansha|ja|山川出版社}}, 2003. {{ill|Imai clan|ja|今井氏#その他の今井氏}} (Samurai) }} '''Ochi clan''' (越智氏) refers to a {{ill|clan|ja|氏族}} with the name "Ochi." The Ochi clan was one of the prominent {{ill|Gōzoku|lt=local ruling clans|ja|豪族}} in ancient {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}, originating in {{ill|Iyo Province|ja|伊予国}} (present-day {{ill|Ehime Prefecture|ja|愛媛県}}).{{cite kotobank|word=越智氏|author=山内 譲|encyclopedia=改訂新版 {{ill|World Encyclopedia|ja|世界大百科事典}}|accessdate=2024-03-10}} Some theories link the clan's origins to its association with the development of the {{ill|Nankaidō|ja|南海道}} region, but this connection remains unverified. == Overview == The Ochi clan is said to have originated in {{ill|Ochi-go|ja|越智郷}} (modern {{ill|Kokubu, Imabari|ja|今治市国分}}). During the late 4th century, the establishment of the {{ill|Kuni no miyatsuko|ja|国造}} system by the Yamato court led to the creation of five Kuni no miyatsuko in western Ehime, including the Ochi clan. The clan’s founder is traditionally considered {{ill|Ochinomikoto|ja|小致命}}, grandson of Ōshinkawa no Mikoto of the {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}} lineage (『{{ill|Kuni no miyatsuko no Hongi|ja|国造本紀}}』). According to the 『{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}』, Ochi no Atai (later Ochi Sukune) was a {{ill|Divine lineage|ja|神別}} clan descending from {{ill|Nigihayahi|ja|饒速日命}}.{{cite web|url=https://www.digital.archives.go.jp/file/1256162.html|title=Shinsen Shōjiroku|website=National Archives of Japan|accessdate=2024-03-10}} During the early 8th century, the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} reorganized the administrative divisions into fourteen districts, designating the former Ochi Kuni no Miyatsuko territory as "Ochi District." The district became the site of Iyo’s {{ill|provincial government|ja|国府}} and {{ill|provincial temple|ja|国分寺}}. Archaeological investigations near Kokubu have suggested a connection between the Ochi clan and the provincial government, but conclusive evidence is lacking. The earliest recorded figure of the Ochi clan is Ochi no Atai, a district governor of Ochi District. According to the 『{{ill|Nihon Ryōiki|ja|日本霊異記}}』, he was captured during the {{ill|Battle of Baekgang|ja|白村江の戦い}} but returned safely thanks to the blessings of {{ill|Kannon|ja|観音菩薩}}, to whom he later dedicated a temple. While the location of this ancient temple remains unknown, it reflects an effort to reassert local authority through Buddhism. In the history of Iyo, the first documented Ochi clan members were Ochi no Atai Asukamaro and Ochi no Atai Kunimaru, who were promoted in 767 ({{ill|Jingō-keiun|ja|神護景雲}} 1) for their tribute offerings. Later, Ochi Mochitada received official recognition in 948 ({{ill|Tenryaku|ja|天暦}} 2) for subduing pirates during the {{ill|Jōhei Tengyō War|ja|承平天慶の乱}}. Despite occasional mentions of the Ochi clan in historical records, the clan disappeared from Iyo’s political scene after Ochi Tamemori’s appointment as a military commissioner in 1002 ({{ill|Chōhō|ja|長保}} 4). == Legacy and Decline == The Ochi clan’s decline is marked by the increasing influence of the {{ill|Kōno clan|ja|河野氏}} in Iyo Province. While sources like 『{{ill|Yoshōki|ja|予章記}}』 provide genealogies linking the Ochi and Kōno clans, these accounts are often embellished with myths, such as tracing the Ochi lineage to Emperor Kōrei’s grandson. Such claims have been met with skepticism due to their lack of historical credibility. According to the {{ill|Ōyamazumi Shrine|ja|大山祇神社}} in modern {{ill|Ōmishima, Imabari|ja|今治市大三島町}}, the Ochi clan became the shrine’s hereditary priests, later adopting the surname {{ill|Mishima|ja|三島家}}. Today, "Ochi" remains a common surname in Imabari City. Notably, during the early Meiji era, documents recorded political leader {{ill|Itō Hirobumi|ja|伊藤博文}} as "Ochi Sukune Hirobumi." == References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist|24em}} == Bibliography == * ''History of Ehime Prefecture – Prefectural History 38'', {{ill|Yamakawa Shuppansha|ja|山川出版社}}, 2003. ISBN 4-634-32380-X * {{Citation|和書|last=丹羽|first=基二|author-link=丹羽基二|others=[[樋口清之]]監修|date=1970-7|title=Surnames: The Definitive Study of Japanese Family Names|publisher=[[Akita {{pp-vandalism|small=yes}} {{otheruses|the Ochi clan of Iyo Province|a branch of the Yamato Minamoto clan|Ochi clan (Yamato Province)}} {{Japanese clans (ancient clans) | Name = Ochi clan | Image = | Clan name = Ochi [[Sukune]] | Founder = {{ill|Ochinomikoto|ja|小致命}}
({{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}} lineage, {{ill|Ochi Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|小市国造}}The Ochi clan descends from Mononobe’s Ochinomikoto, who was appointed as the Ochi Kuni no Miyatsuko (Governor of Ochi County) and controlled Ochi District under the title "Atai." In November of 835 ({{ill|Jōwa|ja|承和 (日本)}} 2), his descendant Ochi Hiroshige was granted the surname Sukune from Atai (『{{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}}』). {{Sfn|丹羽|1970|p=355}})
Alternatively, Ochiō
({{ill|Emperor Kōrei|ja|孝霊天皇}}’s third prince, {{ill|Prince Iyo|ja|伊予皇子}}’s third son?) | Origin = | Ancestor = | Type = {{ill|Divine lineage|ja|神別}}
Alternatively {{ill|Imperial lineage|ja|皇別}} | Hometown = {{ill|Iyo Province|ja|伊予国}}, {{ill|Ochi-go|ja|越智郷}} | Figures = | Descendants = {{ill|Kōno clan|ja|河野氏}} (Samurai)
{{ill|Yano clan|ja|矢野氏}} (Samurai)
{{ill|Iyo Tachibana clan|ja|橘氏 (伊予国)}} (Samurai)
{{ill|Mishima family|ja|三島家 (薩摩藩士の家系)}} (Samurai)
{{ill|Nii clan|ja|新居氏}} (Samurai)
{{ill|Imai clan|ja|今井氏#その他の今井氏}} (Samurai) }} '''Ochi clan''' (越智氏) refers to a {{ill|clan|ja|氏族}} with the name "Ochi." The Ochi clan was one of the prominent {{ill|Gōzoku|lt=local ruling clans|ja|豪族}} in ancient {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}, originating in {{ill|Iyo Province|ja|伊予国}} (present-day {{ill|Ehime Prefecture|ja|愛媛県}}).{{cite kotobank|word=越智氏|author=山内 譲|encyclopedia=改訂新版 {{ill|World Encyclopedia|ja|世界大百科事典}}|accessdate=2024-03-10}} Some theories link the clan's origins to its association with the development of the {{ill|Nankaidō|ja|南海道}} region, but this connection remains unverified. == Overview == The Ochi clan is said to have originated in {{ill|Ochi-go|ja|越智郷}} (modern {{ill|Kokubu, Imabari|ja|今治市国分}}). During the late 4th century, the establishment of the {{ill|Kuni no miyatsuko|ja|国造}} system by the Yamato court led to the creation of five Kuni no miyatsuko in western Ehime, including the Ochi clan. The clan’s founder is traditionally considered {{ill|Ochinomikoto|ja|小致命}}, grandson of Ōshinkawa no Mikoto of the {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}} lineage (『{{ill|Kuni no miyatsuko no Hongi|ja|国造本紀}}』). According to the 『{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}』, Ochi no Atai (later Ochi Sukune) was a {{ill|Divine lineage|ja|神別}} clan descending from {{ill|Nigihayahi|ja|饒速日命}}.{{cite web|url=https://www.digital.archives.go.jp/file/1256162.html|title=Shinsen Shōjiroku|website=National Archives of Japan|accessdate=2024-03-10}} During the early 8th century, the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} reorganized the administrative divisions into fourteen districts, designating the former Ochi Kuni no Miyatsuko territory as "Ochi District." The district became the site of Iyo’s {{ill|provincial government|ja|国府}} and {{ill|provincial temple|ja|国分寺}}. Archaeological investigations near Kokubu have suggested a connection between the Ochi clan and the provincial government, but conclusive evidence is lacking. The earliest recorded figure of the Ochi clan is Ochi no Atai, a district governor of Ochi District. According to the 『{{ill|Nihon Ryōiki|ja|日本霊異記}}』, he was captured during the {{ill|Battle of Baekgang|ja|白村江の戦い}} but returned safely thanks to the blessings of {{ill|Kannon|ja|観音菩薩}}, to whom he later dedicated a temple. While the location of this ancient temple remains unknown, it reflects an effort to reassert local authority through Buddhism. In the history of Iyo, the first documented Ochi clan members were Ochi no Atai Asukamaro and Ochi no Atai Kunimaru, who were promoted in 767 ({{ill|Jingō-keiun|ja|神護景雲}} 1) for their tribute offerings. Later, Ochi Mochitada received official recognition in 948 ({{ill|Tenryaku|ja|天暦}} 2) for subduing pirates during the {{ill|Jōhei Tengyō War|ja|承平天慶の乱}}. Despite occasional mentions of the Ochi clan in historical records, the clan disappeared from Iyo’s political scene after Ochi Tamemori’s appointment as a military commissioner in 1002 ({{ill|Chōhō|ja|長保}} 4). == Legacy and Decline == The Ochi clan’s decline is marked by the increasing influence of the {{ill|Kōno clan|ja|河野氏}} in Iyo Province. While sources like 『{{ill|Yoshōki|ja|予章記}}』 provide genealogies linking the Ochi and Kōno clans, these accounts are often embellished with myths, such as tracing the Ochi lineage to Emperor Kōrei’s grandson. Such claims have been met with skepticism due to their lack of historical credibility. According to the {{ill|Ōyamazumi Shrine|ja|大山祇神社}} in modern {{ill|Ōmishima, Imabari|ja|今治市大三島町}}, the Ochi clan became the shrine’s hereditary priests, later adopting the surname {{ill|Mishima|ja|三島家}}. Today, "Ochi" remains a common surname in Imabari City. Notably, during the early Meiji era, documents recorded political leader {{ill|Itō Hirobumi|ja|伊藤博文}} as "Ochi Sukune Hirobumi." == References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist|24em}} == Bibliography == * ''History of Ehime Prefecture – Prefectural History 38'', {{ill|Yamakawa Shuppansha|ja|山川出版社}}, 2003. ISBN 4-634-32380-X * {{Citation|和書|last=丹羽|first=基二|author-link=丹羽基二|others=[[樋口清之]]監修|date=1970-7|title=Surnames: The Definitive Study of Japanese Family Names|publisher=[[Akita Shoten]]|isbn=4253002099|ref=harv}} == Related Articles == {{div col||10em}} * {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}} * {{ill|Takaichi clan|ja|高市氏}} * {{ill|Nii clan|ja|新居氏}} * {{ill|Bekku clan|ja|別宮氏}} * {{ill|Kōno clan|ja|河野氏}} * {{ill|Iyo Tachibana clan|ja|橘氏 (伊予国)}} * {{ill|Yoshōki|ja|予章記}} * {{ill|Ochi clan (Yamato Province)|ja|越智氏 (大和国)}} {{div col end}} == External Links == * revision.text:
{{pp-vandalism|small=yes}} {{otheruses|the Ochi clan of Iyo Province|a branch of the Yamato Minamoto clan|Ochi clan (Yamato Province)}} {{Japanese clans (ancient clans) | Name = Ochi clan | Image = | Clan name = Ochi [[Sukune]] | Founder = {{ill|Ochinomikoto|ja|小致命}}
({{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}} lineage, {{ill|Ochi Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|小市国造}}The Ochi clan descends from Mononobe’s Ochinomikoto, who was appointed as the Ochi Kuni no Miyatsuko (Governor of Ochi County) and controlled Ochi District under the title "Atai." In November of 835 ({{ill|Jōwa|ja|承和 (日本)}} 2), his descendant Ochi Hiroshige was granted the surname Sukune from Atai (『{{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}}』). {{Sfn|丹羽|1970|p=355}})
Alternatively, Ochiō
({{ill|Emperor Kōrei|ja|孝霊天皇}}’s third prince, {{ill|Prince Iyo|ja|伊予皇子}}’s third son?) | Origin = | Ancestor = | Type = {{ill|Divine lineage|ja|神別}}
Alternatively {{ill|Imperial lineage|ja|皇別}} | Hometown = {{ill|Iyo Province|ja|伊予国}}, {{ill|Ochi-go|ja|越智郷}} | Figures = | Descendants = {{ill|Kōno clan|ja|河野氏}} (Samurai)
{{ill|Yano clan|ja|矢野氏}} (Samurai)
{{ill|Iyo Tachibana clan|ja|橘氏 (伊予国)}} (Samurai)
{{ill|Mishima family|ja|三島家 (薩摩藩士の家系)}} (Samurai)
{{ill|Nii clan|ja|新居氏}} (Samurai)
{{ill|Imai clan|ja|今井氏#その他の今井氏}} (Samurai) }} '''Ochi clan''' (越智氏) refers to a {{ill|clan|ja|氏族}} with the name "Ochi." The Ochi clan was one of the prominent {{ill|Gōzoku|lt=local ruling clans|ja|豪族}} in ancient {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}, originating in {{ill|Iyo Province|ja|伊予国}} (present-day {{ill|Ehime Prefecture|ja|愛媛県}}).{{cite kotobank|word=越智氏|author=山内 譲|encyclopedia=改訂新版 {{ill|World Encyclopedia|ja|世界大百科事典}}|accessdate=2024-03-10}} Some theories link the clan's origins to its association with the development of the {{ill|Nankaidō|ja|南海道}} region, but this connection remains unverified. == Overview == The Ochi clan is said to have originated in {{ill|Ochi-go|ja|越智郷}} (modern {{ill|Kokubu, Imabari|ja|今治市国分}}). During the late 4th century, the establishment of the {{ill|Kuni no miyatsuko|ja|国造}} system by the Yamato court led to the creation of five Kuni no miyatsuko in western Ehime, including the Ochi clan. The clan’s founder is traditionally considered {{ill|Ochinomikoto|ja|小致命}}, grandson of Ōshinkawa no Mikoto of the {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}} lineage (『{{ill|Kuni no miyatsuko no Hongi|ja|国造本紀}}』). According to the 『{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}』, Ochi no Atai (later Ochi Sukune) was a {{ill|Divine lineage|ja|神別}} clan descending from {{ill|Nigihayahi|ja|饒速日命}}.{{cite web|url=https://www.digital.archives.go.jp/file/1256162.html|title=Shinsen Shōjiroku|website=National Archives of Japan|accessdate=2024-03-10}} During the early 8th century, the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} reorganized the administrative divisions into fourteen districts, designating the former Ochi Kuni no Miyatsuko territory as "Ochi District." The district became the site of Iyo’s {{ill|provincial government|ja|国府}} and {{ill|provincial temple|ja|国分寺}}. Archaeological investigations near Kokubu have suggested a connection between the Ochi clan and the provincial government, but conclusive evidence is lacking. The earliest recorded figure of the Ochi clan is Ochi no Atai, a district governor of Ochi District. According to the 『{{ill|Nihon Ryōiki|ja|日本霊異記}}』, he was captured during the {{ill|Battle of Baekgang|ja|白村江の戦い}} but returned safely thanks to the blessings of {{ill|Kannon|ja|観音菩薩}}, to whom he later dedicated a temple. While the location of this ancient temple remains unknown, it reflects an effort to reassert local authority through Buddhism. In the history of Iyo, the first documented Ochi clan members were Ochi no Atai Asukamaro and Ochi no Atai Kunimaru, who were promoted in 767 ({{ill|Jingō-keiun|ja|神護景雲}} 1) for their tribute offerings. Later, Ochi Mochitada received official recognition in 948 ({{ill|Tenryaku|ja|天暦}} 2) for subduing pirates during the {{ill|Jōhei Tengyō War|ja|承平天慶の乱}}. Despite occasional mentions of the Ochi clan in historical records, the clan disappeared from Iyo’s political scene after Ochi Tamemori’s appointment as a military commissioner in 1002 ({{ill|Chōhō|ja|長保}} 4). == Legacy and Decline == The Ochi clan’s decline is marked by the increasing influence of the {{ill|Kōno clan|ja|河野氏}} in Iyo Province. While sources like 『{{ill|Yoshōki|ja|予章記}}』 provide genealogies linking the Ochi and Kōno clans, these accounts are often embellished with myths, such as tracing the Ochi lineage to Emperor Kōrei’s grandson. Such claims have been met with skepticism due to their lack of historical credibility. According to the {{ill|Ōyamazumi Shrine|ja|大山祇神社}} in modern {{ill|Ōmishima, Imabari|ja|今治市大三島町}}, the Ochi clan became the shrine’s hereditary priests, later adopting the surname {{ill|Mishima|ja|三島家}}. Today, "Ochi" remains a common surname in Imabari City. Notably, during the early Meiji era, documents recorded political leader {{ill|Itō Hirobumi|ja|伊藤博文}} as "Ochi Sukune Hirobumi." == References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist|24em}} == Bibliography == * ''History of Ehime Prefecture – Prefectural History 38'', {{ill|Yamakawa Shuppansha|ja|山川出版社}}, 2003. ISBN 4-634-32380-X * {{Citation|和書|last=丹羽|first=基二|author-link=丹羽基二|others=[[樋口清之]]監修|date=1970-7|title=Surnames: The Definitive Study of Japanese Family Names|publisher=[[Akita Shoten]]|isbn=4253002099|ref=harv}} == Related Articles == {{div col||10em}} * {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}} * {{ill|Takaichi clan|ja|高市氏}} * {{ill|Nii clan|ja|新居氏}} * {{ill|Bekku clan|ja|別宮氏}} * {{ill|Kōno clan|ja|河野氏}} * {{ill|Iyo Tachibana clan|ja|橘氏 (伊予国)}} * {{ill|Yoshōki|ja|予章記}} * {{ill|Ochi clan (Yamato Province)|ja|越智氏 (大和国)}} {{div col end}} == External Links == * {{Wayback|url=http://www17.ocn.ne.jp/~kanada/1234-7-15.html |title=Study on the Ochi Clan |date=20080105084406}} * [http://www2.harimaya.com/sengoku/html/oti_k.html Bushi Genealogies — Ochi Clan] While sources like 『{{ill|Yoshōki|ja|予章記}}』 provide genealogies linking the Ochi and Kōno clans, these accounts are often embellished with myths, such as tracing the Ochi lineage to Emperor Kōrei’s grandson. Such claims have been met with skepticism due to their lack of historical credibility. According to the {{ill|Ōyamazumi Shrine|ja|大山祇神社}} in modern {{ill|Ōmishima, Imabari|ja|今治市大三島町}}, the Ochi clan became the shrine’s hereditary priests, later adopting the surname {{ill|Mishima|ja|三島家}}. Today, "Ochi" remains a common surname in Imabari City. Notably, during the early Meiji era, documents recorded political leader {{ill|Itō Hirobumi|ja|伊藤博文}} as "Ochi Sukune Hirobumi." == References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist|24em}} == Bibliography == * ''History of Ehime Prefecture – Prefectural History 38'', {{ill|Yamakawa Shuppansha|ja|山川出版社}}, 2003. ISBN 4-634-32380-X * {{Citation|和書|last=丹羽|first=基二|author-link=丹羽基二|others=[[樋口清之]]監修|date=1970-7|title=Surnames: The Definitive Study of Japanese Family Names|publisher=[[Akita Shoten]]|isbn=4253002099|ref=harv}} == Related Articles == {{div col||10em}} * {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}} * {{ill|Takaichi clan|ja|高市氏}} * {{ill|Nii clan|ja|新居氏}} * {{ill|Bekku clan|ja|別宮氏}} * {{ill|Kōno clan|ja|河野氏}} * {{ill|Iyo Tachibana clan|ja|橘氏 (伊予国)}} * {{ill|Yoshōki|ja|予章記}} * {{ill|Ochi clan (Yamato Province)|ja|越智氏 (大和国)}} {{div col end}} == External Links == * {{Wayback|url=http://www17.ocn.ne.jp/~kanada/1234-7-15.html |title=Study on the Ochi Clan |date=20080105084406}} * [http://www2.harimaya.com/sengoku/html/oti_k.html Bushi Genealogies — Ochi Clan] revision.text:
{{pp-vandalism|small=yes}} {{otheruses|the Ochi clan of Iyo Province|a branch of the Yamato Minamoto clan|Ochi clan (Yamato Province)}} {{Japanese clans (ancient clans) | Name = Ochi clan | Image = | Clan name = Ochi [[Sukune]] | Founder = {{ill|Ochinomikoto|ja|小致命}}
({{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}} lineage, {{ill|Ochi Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|小市国造}}The Ochi clan descends from Mononobe’s Ochinomikoto, who was appointed as the Ochi Kuni no Miyatsuko (Governor of Ochi County) and controlled Ochi District under the title "Atai." In November of 835 ({{ill|Jōwa|ja|承和 (日本)}} 2), his descendant Ochi Hiroshige was granted the surname Sukune from Atai (『{{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}}』). {{Sfn|丹羽|1970|p=355}})
Alternatively, Ochiō
({{ill|Emperor Kōrei|ja|孝霊天皇}}’s third prince, {{ill|Prince Iyo|ja|伊予皇子}}’s third son?) | Origin = | Ancestor = | Type = {{ill|Divine lineage|ja|神別}}
Alternatively {{ill|Imperial lineage|ja|皇別}} | Hometown = {{ill|Iyo Province|ja|伊予国}}, {{ill|Ochi-go|ja|越智郷}} | Figures = | Descendants = {{ill|Kōno clan|ja|河野氏}} (Samurai)
{{ill|Yano clan|ja|矢野氏}} (Samurai)
{{ill|Iyo Tachibana clan|ja|橘氏 (伊予国)}} (Samurai)
{{ill|Mishima family|ja|三島家 (薩摩藩士の家系)}} (Samurai)
{{ill|Nii clan|ja|新居氏}} (Samurai)
{{ill|Imai clan|ja|今井氏#その他の今井氏}} (Samurai) }} '''Ochi clan''' (越智氏) refers to a {{ill|clan|ja|氏族}} with the name "Ochi." The Ochi clan was one of the prominent {{ill|Gōzoku|lt=local ruling clans|ja|豪族}} in ancient {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}, originating in {{ill|Iyo Province|ja|伊予国}} (present-day {{ill|Ehime Prefecture|ja|愛媛県}}).{{cite kotobank|word=越智氏|author=山内 譲|encyclopedia=改訂新版 {{ill|World Encyclopedia|ja|世界大百科事典}}|accessdate=2024-03-10}} Some theories link the clan's origins to its association with the development of the {{ill|Nankaidō|ja|南海道}} region, but this connection remains unverified. == Overview == The Ochi clan is said to have originated in {{ill|Ochi-go|ja|越智郷}} (modern {{ill|Kokubu, Imabari|ja|今治市国分}}). During the late 4th century, the establishment of the {{ill|Kuni no miyatsuko|ja|国造}} system by the Yamato court led to the creation of five Kuni no miyatsuko in western Ehime, including the Ochi clan. The clan’s founder is traditionally considered {{ill|Ochinomikoto|ja|小致命}}, grandson of Ōshinkawa no Mikoto of the {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}} lineage (『{{ill|Kuni no miyatsuko no Hongi|ja|国造本紀}}』). According to the 『{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}』, Ochi no Atai (later Ochi Sukune) was a {{ill|Divine lineage|ja|神別}} clan descending from {{ill|Nigihayahi|ja|饒速日命}}.{{cite web|url=https://www.digital.archives.go.jp/file/1256162.html|title=Shinsen Shōjiroku|website=National Archives of Japan|accessdate=2024-03-10}} During the early 8th century, the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} reorganized the administrative divisions into fourteen districts, designating the former Ochi Kuni no Miyatsuko territory as "Ochi District." The district became the site of Iyo’s {{ill|provincial government|ja|国府}} and {{ill|provincial temple|ja|国分寺}}. Archaeological investigations near Kokubu have suggested a connection between the Ochi clan and the provincial government, but conclusive evidence is lacking. The earliest recorded figure of the Ochi clan is Ochi no Atai, a district governor of Ochi District. According to the 『{{ill|Nihon Ryōiki|ja|日本霊異記}}』, he was captured during the {{ill|Battle of Baekgang|ja|白村江の戦い}} but returned safely thanks to the blessings of {{ill|Kannon|ja|観音菩薩}}, to whom he later dedicated a temple. While the location of this ancient temple remains unknown, it reflects an effort to reassert local authority through Buddhism. In the history of Iyo, the first documented Ochi clan members were Ochi no Atai Asukamaro and Ochi no Atai Kunimaru, who were promoted in 767 ({{ill|Jingō-keiun|ja|神護景雲}} 1) for their tribute offerings. Later, Ochi Mochitada received official recognition in 948 ({{ill|Tenryaku|ja|天暦}} 2) for subduing pirates during the {{ill|Jōhei Tengyō War|ja|承平天慶の乱}}. Despite occasional mentions of the Ochi clan in historical records, the clan disappeared from Iyo’s political scene after Ochi Tamemori’s appointment as a military commissioner in 1002 ({{ill|Chōhō|ja|長保}} 4). == Legacy and Decline == The Ochi clan’s decline is marked by the increasing influence of the {{ill|Kōno clan|ja|河野氏}} in Iyo Province. While sources like 『{{ill|Yoshōki|ja|予章記}}』 provide genealogies linking the Ochi and Kōno clans, these accounts are often embellished with myths, such as tracing the Ochi lineage to Emperor Kōrei’s grandson. Such claims have been met with skepticism due to their lack of historical credibility. According to the {{ill|Ōyamazumi Shrine|ja|大山祇神社}} in modern {{ill|Ōmishima, Imabari|ja|今治市大三島町}}, the Ochi clan became the shrine’s hereditary priests, later adopting the surname {{ill|Mishima|ja|三島家}}. Today, "Ochi" remains a common surname in Imabari City. Notably, during the early Meiji era, documents recorded political leader {{ill|Itō Hirobumi|ja|伊藤博文}} as "Ochi Sukune Hirobumi." == References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist|24em}} == Bibliography == * ''History of Ehime Prefecture – Prefectural History 38'', {{ill|Yamakawa Shuppansha|ja|山川出版社}}, 2003. ISBN 4-634-32380-X * {{Citation|和書|last=丹羽|first=基二|author-link=丹羽基二|others=[[樋口清之]]監修|date=1970-7|title=Surnames: The Definitive Study of Japanese Family Names|publisher=[[Akita Shoten]]|isbn=4253002099|ref=harv}} == Related Articles == {{div col||10em}} * {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}} * {{ill|Takaichi clan|ja|高市氏}} * {{ill|Nii clan|ja|新居氏}} * {{ill|Bekku clan|ja|別宮氏}} * {{ill|Kōno clan|ja|河野氏}} * {{ill|Iyo Tachibana clan|ja|橘氏 (伊予国)}} * {{ill|Yoshōki|ja|予章記}} * {{ill|Ochi clan (Yamato Province)|ja|越智氏 (大和国)}} {{div col end}} == External Links == * {{Wayback|url=http://www17.ocn.ne.jp/~kanada/1234-7-15.html |title=Study on the Ochi Clan |date=20080105084406}} * [http://www2.harimaya.com/sengoku/html/oti_k.html Bushi Genealogies — Ochi Clan] The Ochi clan was one of the prominent {{ill|Gōzoku|lt=local ruling clans|ja|豪族}} in ancient {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}, originating in {{ill|Iyo Province|ja|伊予国}} (present-day {{ill|Ehime Prefecture|ja|愛媛県}}).{{cite kotobank|word=越智氏|author=山内 譲|encyclopedia=改訂新版 {{ill|World Encyclopedia|ja|世界大百科事典}}|accessdate=2024-03-10}} Some theories link the clan's origins to its association with the development of the {{ill|Nankaidō|ja|南海道}} region, but this connection remains unverified. == Overview == The Ochi clan is said to have originated in {{ill|Ochi-go|ja|越智郷}} (modern {{ill|Kokubu, Imabari|ja|今治市国分}}). During the late 4th century, the establishment of the {{ill|Kuni no miyatsuko|ja|国造}} system by the Yamato court led to the creation of five Kuni no miyatsuko in western Ehime, including the Ochi clan. The clan’s founder is traditionally considered {{ill|Ochinomikoto|ja|小致命}}, grandson of Ōshinkawa no Mikoto of the {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}} lineage (『{{ill|Kuni no miyatsuko no Hongi|ja|国造本紀}}』). According to the 『{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}』, Ochi no Atai (later Ochi Sukune) was a {{ill|Divine lineage|ja|神別}} clan descending from {{ill|Nigihayahi|ja|饒速日命}}.{{cite web|url=https://www.digital.archives.go.jp/file/1256162.html|title=Shinsen Shōjiroku|website=National Archives of Japan|accessdate=2024-03-10}} During the early 8th century, the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} reorganized the administrative divisions into fourteen districts, designating the former Ochi Kuni no Miyatsuko territory as "Ochi District." The district became the site of Iyo’s {{ill|provincial government|ja|国府}} and {{ill|provincial temple|ja|国分寺}}. Archaeological investigations near Kokubu have suggested a connection between the Ochi clan and the provincial government, but conclusive evidence is lacking. The earliest recorded figure of the Ochi clan is Ochi no Atai, a district governor of Ochi District. According to the 『{{ill|Nihon Ryōiki|ja|日本霊異記}}』, he was captured during the {{ill|Battle of Baekgang|ja|白村江の戦い}} but returned safely thanks to the blessings of {{ill|Kannon|ja|観音菩薩}}, to whom he later dedicated a temple. While the location of this ancient temple remains unknown, it reflects an effort to reassert local authority through Buddhism. In the history of Iyo, the first documented Ochi clan members were Ochi no Atai Asukamaro and Ochi no Atai Kunimaru, who were promoted in 767 ({{ill|Jingō-keiun|ja|神護景雲}} 1) for their tribute offerings. Later, Ochi Mochitada received official recognition in 948 ({{ill|Tenryaku|ja|天暦}} 2) for subduing pirates during the {{ill|Jōhei Tengyō War|ja|承平天慶の乱}}. Despite occasional mentions of the Ochi clan in historical records, the clan disappeared from Iyo’s political scene after Ochi Tamemori’s appointment as a military commissioner in 1002 ({{ill|Chōhō|ja|長保}} 4). == Legacy and Decline == The Ochi clan’s decline is marked by the increasing influence of the {{ill|Kōno clan|ja|河野氏}} in Iyo Province. While sources like 『{{ill|Yoshōki|ja|予章記}}』 provide genealogies linking the Ochi and Kōno clans, these accounts are often embellished with myths, such as tracing the Ochi lineage to Emperor Kōrei’s grandson. Such claims have been met with skepticism due to their lack of historical credibility. According to the {{ill|Ōyamazumi Shrine|ja|大山祇神社}} in modern {{ill|Ōmishima, Imabari|ja|今治市大三島町}}, the Ochi clan became the shrine’s hereditary priests, later adopting the surname {{ill|Mishima|ja|三島家}}. Today, "Ochi" remains a common surname in Imabari City. Notably, during the early Meiji era, documents recorded political leader {{ill|Itō Hirobumi|ja|伊藤博文}} as "Ochi Sukune Hirobumi." == References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist|24em}} == Bibliography == * ''History of Ehime Prefecture – Prefectural History 38'', {{ill|Yamakawa Shuppansha|ja|山川出版社}}, 2003. ({{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}} lineage, {{ill|Ochi Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|小市国造}}The Ochi clan descends from Mononobe’s Ochinomikoto, who was appointed as the Ochi Kuni no Miyatsuko (Governor of Ochi County) and controlled Ochi District under the title "Atai." In November of 835 ({{ill|Jōwa|ja|承和 (日本)}} 2), his descendant Ochi Hiroshige was granted the surname Sukune from Atai (『{{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}}』). {{Sfn|丹羽|1970|p=355}})
Alternatively, Ochiō
({{ill|Emperor Kōrei|ja|孝霊天皇}}’s third prince, {{ill|Prince Iyo|ja|伊予皇子}}’s third son?) | Origin = | Ancestor = | Type = {{ill|Divine lineage|ja|神別}}
Alternatively {{ill|Imperial lineage|ja|皇別}} | Hometown = {{ill|Iyo Province|ja|伊予国}}, {{ill|Ochi-go|ja|越智郷}} | Figures = | Descendants = {{ill|Kōno clan|ja|河野氏}} (Samurai)
{{ill|Yano clan|ja|矢野氏}} (Samurai)
{{ill|Iyo Tachibana clan|ja|橘氏 (伊予国)}} (Samurai)
{{ill|Mishima family|ja|三島家 (薩摩藩士の家系)}} (Samurai)
{{ill|Nii clan|ja|新居氏}} (Samurai)
{{ill|Imai clan|ja|今井氏#その他の今井氏}} (Samurai) }} '''Ochi clan''' (越智氏) refers to a {{ill|clan|ja|氏族}} with the name "Ochi." The Ochi clan was one of the prominent {{ill|Gōzoku|lt=local ruling clans|ja|豪族}} in ancient {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}, originating in {{ill|Iyo Province|ja|伊予国}} (present-day {{ill|Ehime Prefecture|ja|愛媛県}}).{{cite kotobank|word=越智氏|author=山内 譲|encyclopedia=改訂新版 {{ill|World Encyclopedia|ja|世界大百科事典}}|accessdate=2024-03-10}} Some theories link the clan's origins to its association with the development of the {{ill|Nankaidō|ja|南海道}} region, but this connection remains unverified. == Overview == The Ochi clan is said to have originated in {{ill|Ochi-go|ja|越智郷}} (modern {{ill|Kokubu, Imabari|ja|今治市国分}}). During the late 4th century, the establishment of the {{ill|Kuni no miyatsuko|ja|国造}} system by the Yamato court led to the creation of five Kuni no miyatsuko in western Ehime, including the Ochi clan. The clan’s founder is traditionally considered {{ill|Ochinomikoto|ja|小致命}}, grandson of Ōshinkawa no Mikoto of the {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}} lineage (『{{ill|Kuni no miyatsuko no Hongi|ja|国造本紀}}』). According to the 『{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}』, Ochi no Atai (later Ochi Sukune) was a {{ill|Divine lineage|ja|神別}} clan descending from {{ill|Nigihayahi|ja|饒速日命}}.{{cite web|url=https://www.digital.archives.go.jp/file/1256162.html|title=Shinsen Shōjiroku|website=National Archives of Japan|accessdate=2024-03-10}} During the early 8th century, the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} reorganized the administrative divisions into fourteen districts, designating the former Ochi Kuni no Miyatsuko territory as "Ochi District." The district became the site of Iyo’s {{ill|provincial government|ja|国府}} and {{ill|provincial temple|ja|国分寺}}. Archaeological investigations near Kokubu have suggested a connection between the Ochi clan and the provincial government, but conclusive evidence is lacking. The earliest recorded figure of the Ochi clan is Ochi no Atai, a district governor of Ochi District. According to the 『{{ill|Nihon Ryōiki|ja|日本霊異記}}』, he was captured during the {{ill|Battle of Baekgang|ja|白村江の戦い}} but returned safely thanks to the blessings of {{ill|Kannon|ja|観音菩薩}}, to whom he later dedicated a temple. While the location of this ancient temple remains unknown, it reflects an effort to reassert local authority through Buddhism. In the history of Iyo, the first documented Ochi clan members were Ochi no Atai Asukamaro and Ochi no Atai Kunimaru, who were promoted in 767 ({{ill|Jingō-keiun|ja|神護景雲}} 1) for their tribute offerings. Later, Ochi Mochitada received official recognition in 948 ({{ill|Tenryaku|ja|天暦}} 2) for subduing pirates during the {{ill|Jōhei Tengyō War|ja|承平天慶の乱}}. Despite occasional mentions of the Ochi clan in historical records, the clan disappeared from Iyo’s political scene after Ochi Tamemori’s appointment as a military commissioner in 1002 ({{ill|Chōhō|ja|長保}} 4). == Legacy and Decline == The Ochi clan’s decline is marked by the increasing influence of the {{ill|Kōno clan|ja|河野氏}} in Iyo Province. While sources like 『{{ill|Yoshōki|ja|予章記}}』 provide genealogies linking the Ochi and Kōno clans, these accounts are often embellished with myths, such as tracing the Ochi lineage to Emperor Kōrei’s grandson. Such claims have been met with skepticism due to their lack of historical credibility. According to the {{ill|Ōyamazumi Shrine|ja|大山祇神社}} in modern {{ill|Ōmishima, Imabari|ja|今治市大三島町}}, the Ochi clan became the shrine’s hereditary priests, later adopting the surname {{ill|Mishima|ja|三島家}}. Today, "Ochi" remains a common surname in Imabari City. Notably, during the early Meiji era, documents recorded political leader {{ill|Itō Hirobumi|ja|伊藤博文}} as "Ochi Sukune Hirobumi." == References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist|24em}} == Bibliography == * ''History of Ehime Prefecture – Prefectural History 38'', {{ill|Yamakawa Shuppansha|ja|山川出版社}}, 2003. ISBN 4-634-32380-X * {{Citation|和書|last=丹羽|first=基二|author-link=丹羽基二|others=[[樋口清之]]監修|date=1970-7|title=Surnames: The Definitive Study of Japanese Family Names|publisher=[[Akita Shoten]]|isbn=4253002099|ref=harv}} == Related Articles == {{div col||10em}} * {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}} * {{ill|Takaichi clan|ja|高市氏}} * {{ill|Nii clan|ja|新居氏}} * {{ill|Bekku clan|ja|別宮氏}} * {{pp-vandalism|small=yes}} {{otheruses|the Ochi clan of Iyo Province|a branch of the Yamato Minamoto clan|Ochi clan (Yamato Province)}} {{Japanese clans (ancient clans) | Name = Ochi clan | Image = | Clan name = Ochi [[Sukune]] | Founder = {{ill|Ochinomikoto|ja|小致命}}
({{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}} lineage, {{ill|Ochi Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|小市国造}}The Ochi clan descends from Mononobe’s Ochinomikoto, who was appointed as the Ochi Kuni no Miyatsuko (Governor of Ochi County) and controlled Ochi District under the title "Atai." In November of 835 ({{ill|Jōwa|ja|承和 (日本)}} 2), his descendant Ochi Hiroshige was granted the surname Sukune from Atai (『{{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}}』). {{Sfn|丹羽|1970|p=355}})
Alternatively, Ochiō
({{ill|Emperor Kōrei|ja|孝霊天皇}}’s third prince, {{ill|Prince Iyo|ja|伊予皇子}}’s third son?) | Origin = | Ancestor = | Type = {{ill|Divine lineage|ja|神別}}
Alternatively {{ill|Imperial lineage|ja|皇別}} | Hometown = {{ill|Iyo Province|ja|伊予国}}, {{ill|Ochi-go|ja|越智郷}} | Figures = | Descendants = {{ill|Kōno clan|ja|河野氏}} (Samurai)
{{ill|Yano clan|ja|矢野氏}} (Samurai)
{{ill|Iyo Tachibana clan|ja|橘氏 (伊予国)}} (Samurai)
{{ill|Mishima family|ja|三島家 (薩摩藩士の家系)}} (Samurai)
{{ill|Nii clan|ja|新居氏}} (Samurai)
{{ill|Imai clan|ja|今井氏#その他の今井氏}} (Samurai) }} '''Ochi clan''' (越智氏) refers to a {{ill|clan|ja|氏族}} with the name "Ochi." The Ochi clan was one of the prominent {{ill|Gōzoku|lt=local ruling clans|ja|豪族}} in ancient {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}, originating in {{ill|Iyo Province|ja|伊予国}} (present-day {{ill|Ehime Prefecture|ja|愛媛県}}).{{cite kotobank|word=越智氏|author=山内 譲|encyclopedia=改訂新版 {{ill|World Encyclopedia|ja|世界大百科事典}}|accessdate=2024-03-10}} Some theories link the clan's origins to its association with the development of the {{ill|Nankaidō|ja|南海道}} region, but this connection remains unverified. == Overview == The Ochi clan is said to have originated in {{ill|Ochi-go|ja|越智郷}} (modern {{ill|Kokubu, Imabari|ja|今治市国分}}). During the late 4th century, the establishment of the {{ill|Kuni no miyatsuko|ja|国造}} system by the Yamato court led to the creation of five Kuni no miyatsuko in western Ehime, including the Ochi clan. The clan’s founder is traditionally considered {{ill|Ochinomikoto|ja|小致命}}, grandson of Ōshinkawa no Mikoto of the {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}} lineage (『{{ill|Kuni no miyatsuko no Hongi|ja|国造本紀}}』). According to the 『{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}』, Ochi no Atai (later Ochi Sukune) was a {{ill|Divine lineage|ja|神別}} clan descending from {{ill|Nigihayahi|ja|饒速日命}}.{{cite web|url=https://www.digital.archives.go.jp/file/1256162.html|title=Shinsen Shōjiroku|website=National Archives of Japan|accessdate=2024-03-10}} During the early 8th century, the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} reorganized the administrative divisions into fourteen districts, designating the former Ochi Kuni no Miyatsuko territory as "Ochi District." The district became the site of Iyo’s {{ill|provincial government|ja|国府}} and {{ill|provincial temple|ja|国分寺}}. Archaeological investigations near Kokubu have suggested a connection between the Ochi clan and the provincial government, but conclusive evidence is lacking. The earliest recorded figure of the Ochi clan is Ochi no Atai, a district governor of Ochi District. According to the 『{{ill|Nihon Ryōiki|ja|日本霊異記}}』, he was captured during the {{ill|Battle of Baekgang|ja|白村江の戦い}} but returned safely thanks to the blessings of {{ill|Kannon|ja|観音菩薩}}, to whom he later dedicated a temple. While the location of this ancient temple remains unknown, it reflects an effort to reassert local authority through Buddhism. In the history of Iyo, the first documented Ochi clan members were Ochi no Atai Asukamaro and Ochi no Atai Kunimaru, who were promoted in 767 ({{ill|Jingō-keiun|ja|神護景雲}} 1) for their tribute offerings. Later, Ochi Mochitada received official recognition in 948 ({{ill|Tenryaku|ja|天暦}} 2) for subduing pirates during the {{ill|Jōhei Tengyō War|ja|承平天慶の乱}}. Despite occasional mentions of the Ochi clan in historical records, the clan disappeared from Iyo’s political scene after Ochi Tamemori’s appointment as a military commissioner in 1002 ({{ill|Chōhō|ja|長保}} 4). == Legacy and Decline == The Ochi clan’s decline is marked by the increasing influence of the {{ill|Kōno clan|ja|河野氏}} in Iyo Province. While sources like 『{{ill|Yoshōki|ja|予章記}}』 provide genealogies linking the Ochi and Kōno clans, these accounts are often embellished with myths, such as tracing the Ochi lineage to Emperor Kōrei’s grandson. Such claims have been met with skepticism due to their lack of historical credibility. According to the {{ill|Ōyamazumi Shrine|ja|大山祇神社}} in modern {{ill|Ōmishima, Imabari|ja|今治市大三島町}}, the Ochi clan became the shrine’s hereditary priests, later adopting the surname {{ill|Mishima|ja|三島家}}. Today, "Ochi" remains a common surname in Imabari City. Notably, during the early Meiji era, documents recorded political leader {{ill|Itō Hirobumi|ja|伊藤博文}} as "Ochi Sukune Hirobumi." == References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist|24em}} == Bibliography == * ''History of Ehime Prefecture – Prefectural History 38'', {{ill|Yamakawa Shuppansha|ja|山川出版社}}, 2003. ISBN 4-634-32380-X * {{Citation|和書|last=丹羽|first=基二|author-link=丹羽基二|others=[[樋口清之]]監修|date=1970-7|title=Surnames: The Definitive Study of Japanese Family Names|publisher=[[Akita Shoten]]|isbn=4253002099|ref=harv}} == Related Articles == {{div col||10em}} * {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}} * {{ill|Takaichi clan|ja|高市氏}} * {{ill|Nii clan|ja|新居氏}} * {{ill|Bekku clan|ja|別宮氏}} * {{ill|Kōno clan|ja|河野氏}} * {{ill|Iyo Tachibana clan|ja|橘氏 (伊予国)}} * {{ill|Yoshōki|ja|予章記}} * {{ill|Ochi clan (Yamato Province)|ja|越智氏 (Yamato Province )}} {{div col end}} == External Links == * According to the {{ill|Ōyamazumi Shrine|ja|大山祇神社}} in modern {{ill|Ōmishima, Imabari|ja|今治市大三島町}}, the Ochi clan became the shrine’s hereditary priests, later adopting the surname {{ill|Mishima|ja|三島家}}. Today, "Ochi" remains a common surname in Imabari City. Notably, during the early Meiji era, documents recorded political leader {{ill|Itō Hirobumi|ja|伊藤博文}} as "Ochi Sukune Hirobumi." == References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist|24em}} == Bibliography == * ''History of Ehime Prefecture – Prefectural History 38'', {{ill|Yamakawa Shuppansha|ja|山川出版社}}, 2003. ISBN 4-634-32380-X * {{Citation|和書|last=丹羽|first=基二|author-link=丹羽基二|others=[[樋口清之]]監修|date=1970-7|title=Surnames: The Definitive Study of Japanese Family Names|publisher=[[Akita Shoten]]|isbn=4253002099|ref=harv}} == Related Articles == {{div col||10em}} * {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}} * {{ill|Takaichi clan|ja|高市氏}} * {{ill|Nii clan|ja|新居氏}} * {{ill|Bekku clan|ja|別宮氏}} * {{ill|Kōno clan|ja|河野氏}} * {{ill|Iyo Tachibana clan|ja|橘氏 (伊予国)}} * {{ill|Yoshōki|ja|予章記}} * {{ill|Ochi clan (Yamato Province)|ja|越智氏 (Yamato Province )}} {{div col end}} == External Links == * {{pp-vandalism|small=yes}} {{otheruses|the Ochi clan of Iyo Province|a branch of the Yamato Minamoto clan|Ochi clan (Yamato Province)}} {{Japanese clans (ancient clans) | Name = Ochi clan | image = | Clan name = Ochi [[Sukune]] | Founder = {{ill|Ochinomikoto|ja|小致命}}
({{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}} lineage, {{ill|Ochi Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|小市国造}}The Ochi clan descends from Mononobe’s Ochinomikoto, who was appointed as the Ochi Kuni no Miyatsuko (Governor of Ochi County) and controlled Ochi District under the title "Atai." In November of 835 ({{ill|Jōwa|ja|承和 (日本)}} 2), his descendant Ochi Hiroshige was granted the surname Sukune from Atai (『{{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}}』). {{Sfn|丹羽|1970|p=355}})
Alternatively, Ochiō
({{ill|Emperor Kōrei|ja|孝霊天皇}}’s third prince, {{ill|Prince Iyo|ja|伊予皇子}}’s third son?) | Origin = | Ancestor = | Type = {{ill|Divine lineage|ja|神別}}
Alternatively {{ill|Imperial lineage|ja|皇別}} | Hometown = {{ill|Iyo Province|ja|伊予国}}, {{ill|Ochi-go|ja|越智郷}} | Figures = | Descendants = {{ill|Kōno clan|ja|河野氏}} (Samurai)
{{ill|Yano clan|ja|矢野氏}} (Samurai)
{{ill|Iyo Tachibana clan|ja|橘氏 (伊予国)}} (Samurai)
{{ill|Mishima family|ja|三島家 (薩摩藩士の家系)}} (Samurai)
{{ill|Nii clan|ja|新居氏}} (Samurai)
{{ill|Imai clan|ja|今井氏#その他の今井氏}} (Samurai) }} '''Ochi clan''' (越智氏) refers to a {{ill|clan|ja|氏族}} with the name "Ochi." The Ochi clan was one of the prominent {{ill|Gōzoku|lt=local ruling clans|ja|豪族}} in ancient {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}, originating in {{ill|Iyo Province|ja|伊予国}} (present-day {{ill|Ehime Prefecture|ja|愛媛県}}).{{cite kotobank|word=越智氏|author=山内 譲|encyclopedia=改訂新版 {{ill|World Encyclopedia|ja|世界大百科事典}}|accessdate=2024-03-10}} Some theories link the clan's origins to its association with the development of the {{ill|Nankaidō|ja|南海道}} region, but this connection remains unverified. == Overview == The Ochi clan is said to have originated in {{ill|Ochi-go|ja|越智郷}} (modern {{ill|Kokubu, Imabari|ja|今治市国分}}). During the late 4th century, the establishment of the {{ill|Kuni no miyatsuko|ja|国造}} system by the Yamato court led to the creation of five Kuni no miyatsuko in western Ehime, including the Ochi clan. The clan’s founder is traditionally considered {{ill|Ochinomikoto|ja|小致命}}, grandson of Ōshinkawa no Mikoto of the {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}} lineage (『{{ill|Kuni no miyatsuko no Hongi|ja|国造本紀}}』). According to the 『{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}』, Ochi no Atai (later Ochi Sukune) was a {{ill|Divine lineage|ja|神別}} clan descending from {{ill|Nigihayahi|ja|饒速日命}}.{{cite web|url=https://www.digital.archives.go.jp/file/1256162.html|title=Shinsen Shōjiroku|website=National Archives of Japan|accessdate=2024-03-10}} During the early 8th century, the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} reorganized the administrative divisions into fourteen districts, designating the former Ochi Kuni no Miyatsuko territory as "Ochi District." The district became the site of Iyo’s {{ill|provincial government|ja|国府}} and {{ill|provincial temple|ja|国分寺}}. Archaeological investigations near Kokubu have suggested a connection between the Ochi clan and the provincial government, but conclusive evidence is lacking. The earliest recorded figure of the Ochi clan is Ochi no Atai, a district governor of Ochi District. According to the 『{{ill|Nihon Ryōiki|ja|日本霊異記}}』, he was captured during the {{ill|Battle of Baekgang|ja|白村江の戦い}} but returned safely thanks to the blessings of {{ill|Kannon|ja|観音菩薩}}, to whom he later dedicated a temple. While the location of this ancient temple remains unknown, it reflects an effort to reassert local authority through Buddhism. In the history of Iyo, the first documented Ochi clan members were Ochi no Atai Asukamaro and Ochi no Atai Kunimaru, who were promoted in 767 ({{ill|Jingō-keiun|ja|神護景雲}} 1) for their tribute offerings. Later, Ochi Mochitada received official recognition in 948 ({{ill|Tenryaku|ja|天暦}} 2) for subduing pirates during the {{ill|Jōhei Tengyō War|ja|承平天慶の乱}}. Despite occasional mentions of the Ochi clan in historical records, the clan disappeared from Iyo’s political scene after Ochi Tamemori’s appointment as a military commissioner in 1002 ({{ill|Chōhō|ja|長保}} 4). == Legacy and Decline == The Ochi clan’s decline is marked by the increasing influence of the {{ill|Kōno clan|ja|河野氏}} in Iyo Province. While sources like 『{{ill|Yoshōki|ja|予章記}}』 provide genealogies linking the Ochi and Kōno clans, these accounts are often embellished with myths, such as tracing the Ochi lineage to Emperor Kōrei’s grandson. Such claims have been met with skepticism due to their lack of historical credibility. According to the {{ill|Ōyamazumi Shrine|ja|大山祇神社}} in modern {{ill|Ōmishima, Imabari|ja|今治市大三島町}}, the Ochi clan became the shrine’s hereditary priests, later adopting the surname {{ill|Mishima|ja|三島家}}. Today, "Ochi" remains a common surname in Imabari City. Notably, during the early Meiji era, documents recorded political leader {{ill|Itō Hirobumi|ja|伊藤博文}} as "Ochi Sukune Hirobumi." == References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist|24em}} == Bibliography == * ''History of Ehime Prefecture – Prefectural History 38'', {{ill|Yamakawa Shuppansha|ja|山川出版社}}, 2003. ISBN 4-634-32380-X * {{Citation|和書|last=丹羽|first=基二|author-link=丹羽基二|others=[[樋口清之]]監修|date=1970-7|title=Surnames: The Definitive Study of Japanese Family Names|publisher=[[Akita Shoten]]|isbn=4253002099|ref=harv}} == Related Articles == {{div col||10em}} * {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}} * {{ill|Takaichi clan|ja|高市氏}} * {{ill|Nii clan|ja|新居氏}} * {{ill|Bekku clan|ja|別宮氏}} * {{ill|Kōno clan|ja|河野氏}} * {{ill|Iyo Tachibana clan|ja|橘氏 (伊予国)}} * {{ill|Yoshōki|ja|予章記}} * {{ill|Ochi clan (Yamato Province)|ja|越智氏 (Yamato Province )}} {{div col end}} == External Links == * {{Wayback|url=http://www17.ocn.ne.jp/~kanada/1234-7-15.html |title=Study on the Ochi Clan |date=20080105084406}} * [http://www2.harimaya.com/sengoku/html/oti_k.html Bushi Genealogies — Ochi Clan] During the late 4th century, the establishment of the {{ill|Kuni no miyatsuko|ja|国造}} system by the Yamato court led to the creation of five Kuni no miyatsuko in western Ehime, including the Ochi clan. The clan’s founder is traditionally considered {{ill|Ochinomikoto|ja|小致命}}, grandson of Ōshinkawa no Mikoto of the {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}} lineage (『{{ill|Kuni no miyatsuko no Hongi|ja|国造本紀}}』). According to the 『{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}』, Ochi no Atai (later Ochi Sukune) was a {{ill|Divine lineage|ja|神別}} clan descending from {{ill|Nigihayahi|ja|饒速日命}}.{{cite web|url=https://www.digital.archives.go.jp/file/1256162.html|title=Shinsen Shōjiroku|website=National Archives of Japan|accessdate=2024-03-10}} During the early 8th century, the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} reorganized the administrative divisions into fourteen districts, designating the former Ochi Kuni no Miyatsuko territory as "Ochi District." The district became the site of Iyo’s {{ill|provincial government|ja|国府}} and {{ill|provincial temple|ja|国分寺}}. Archaeological investigations near Kokubu have suggested a connection between the Ochi clan and the provincial government, but conclusive evidence is lacking. The earliest recorded figure of the Ochi clan is Ochi no Atai, a district governor of Ochi District. According to the 『{{ill|Nihon Ryōiki|ja|日本霊異記}}』, he was captured during the {{ill|Battle of Baekgang|ja|白村江の戦い}} but returned safely thanks to the blessings of {{ill|Kannon|ja|観音菩薩}}, to whom he later dedicated a temple. While the location of this ancient temple remains unknown, it reflects an effort to reassert local authority through Buddhism. In the history of Iyo, the first documented Ochi clan members were Ochi no Atai Asukamaro and Ochi no Atai Kunimaru, who were promoted in 767 ({{ill|Jingō-keiun|ja|神護景雲}} 1) for their tribute offerings. Later, Ochi Mochitada received official recognition in 948 ({{ill|Tenryaku|ja|天暦}} 2) for subduing pirates during the {{ill|Jōhei Tengyō War|ja|承平天慶の乱}}. Despite occasional mentions of the Ochi clan in historical records, the clan disappeared from Iyo’s political scene after Ochi Tamemori’s appointment as a military commissioner in 1002 ({{ill|Chōhō|ja|長保}} 4). == Legacy and Decline == The Ochi clan’s decline is marked by the increasing influence of the {{ill|Kōno clan|ja|河野氏}} in Iyo Province. While sources like 『{{ill|Yoshōki|ja|予章記}}』 provide genealogies linking the Ochi and Kōno clans, these accounts are often embellished with myths, such as tracing the Ochi lineage to Emperor Kōrei’s grandson. Such claims have been met with skepticism due to their lack of historical credibility. According to the {{ill|Ōyamazumi Shrine|ja|大山祇神社}} in modern {{ill|Ōmishima, Imabari|ja|今治市大三島町}}, the Ochi clan became the shrine’s hereditary priests, later adopting the surname {{ill|Mishima|ja|三島家}}. Today, "Ochi" remains a common surname in Imabari City. Notably, during the early Meiji era, documents recorded political leader {{ill|Itō Hirobumi|ja|伊藤博文}} as "Ochi Sukune Hirobumi." == References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist|24em}} == Bibliography == * ''History of Ehime Prefecture – Prefectural History 38'', {{ill|Yamakawa Shuppansha|ja|山川出版社}}, 2003. ISBN 4-634-32380-X * {{Citation|和書|last=丹羽|first=基二|author-link=丹羽基二|others=[[樋口清之]]監修|date=1970-7|title=Surnames: The Definitive Study of Japanese Family Names|publisher=[[Akita Shoten]]|isbn=4253002099|ref=harv}} == Related Articles == {{div col||10em}} * {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}} * {{ill|Takaichi clan|ja|高市氏}} * {{ill|Nii clan|ja|新居氏}} * {{ill|Bekku clan|ja|別宮氏}} * {{ill|Kōno clan|ja|河野氏}} * {{ill|Iyo Tachibana clan|ja|橘氏 (伊予国)}} * {{ill|Yoshōki|ja|予章記}} * {{ill|Ochi clan (Yamato Province)|ja|越智氏 (Yamato Province )}} {{div col end}} == External Links == * {{pp-vandalism|small=yes}} {{otheruses|the Ochi clan of Iyo Province|a branch of the Yamato Minamoto clan|Ochi clan (Yamato Province)}} {{Japanese clans (ancient clans) | Name = Ochi clan | image = | Clan name = Ochi [[Sukune]] | Founder = {{ill|Ochinomikoto|ja|小致命}}
({{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}} lineage, {{ill|Ochi Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|小市国造}}The Ochi clan descends from Mononobe’s Ochinomikoto, who was appointed as the Ochi Kuni no Miyatsuko (Governor of Ochi County) and controlled Ochi District under the title "Atai." In November of 835 ({{ill|Jōwa|ja|承和 (日本)}} 2), his descendant Ochi Hiroshige was granted the surname Sukune from Atai (『{{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}}』). {{Sfn|丹羽|1970|p=355}})
Alternatively, Ochiō
({{ill|Emperor Kōrei|ja|孝霊天皇}}’s third prince, {{ill|Prince Iyo|ja|伊予皇子}}’s third son?) | Origin = | Ancestor = | Type = {{ill|Divine lineage|ja|神別}}
Alternatively {{ill|Imperial lineage|ja|皇別}} | Hometown = {{ill|Iyo Province|ja|伊予国}}, {{ill|Ochi-go|ja|越智郷}} | Figures = | Descendants = {{ill|Kōno clan|ja|河野氏}} (Samurai)
{{ill|Yano clan|ja|矢野氏}} (Samurai)
{{ill|Iyo Tachibana clan|ja|橘氏 (伊予国)}} (Samurai)
{{ill|Mishima family|ja|三島家 (薩摩藩士の家系)}} (Samurai)
{{ill|Nii clan|ja|新居氏}} (Samurai)
{{ill|Imai clan|ja|今井氏#その他の今井氏}} (Samurai) }} '''Ochi clan''' (越智氏) refers to a {{ill|clan|ja|氏族}} with the name "Ochi." The Ochi clan was one of the prominent {{ill|Gōzoku|lt=local ruling clans|ja|豪族}} in ancient {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}, originating in {{ill|Iyo Province|ja|伊予国}} (present-day {{ill|Ehime Prefecture|ja|愛媛県}}).{{cite kotobank|word=越智氏|author=山内 譲|encyclopedia=改訂新版 {{ill|World Encyclopedia|ja|世界大百科事典}}|accessdate=2024-03-10}} Some theories link the clan's origins to its association with the development of the {{ill|Nankaidō|ja|南海道}} region, but this connection remains unverified. == Overview == The Ochi clan is said to have originated in {{ill|Ochi-go|ja|越智郷}} (modern {{ill|Kokubu, Imabari|ja|今治市国分}}). During the late 4th century, the establishment of the {{ill|Kuni no miyatsuko|ja|国造}} system by the Yamato court led to the creation of five Kuni no miyatsuko in western Ehime, including the Ochi clan. The clan’s founder is traditionally considered {{ill|Ochinomikoto|ja|小致命}}, grandson of Ōshinkawa no Mikoto of the {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}} lineage (『{{ill|Kuni no miyatsuko no Hongi|ja|国造本紀}}』). According to the 『{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録}}』, Ochi no Atai (later Ochi Sukune) was a {{ill|Divine lineage|ja|神別}} clan descending from {{ill|Nigihayahi|ja|饒速日命}}.{{cite web|url=https://www.digital.archives.go.jp/file/1256162.html|title=Shinsen Shōjiroku|website=National Archives of Japan|accessdate=2024-03-10}} During the early 8th century, the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} reorganized the administrative divisions into fourteen districts, designating the former Ochi Kuni no Miyatsuko territory as "Ochi District." The district became the site of Iyo’s {{ill|provincial government|ja|国府}} and {{ill|provincial temple|ja|国分寺}}. Archaeological investigations near Kokubu have suggested a connection between the Ochi clan and the provincial government, but conclusive evidence is lacking. The earliest recorded figure of the Ochi clan is Ochi no Atai, a district governor of Ochi District. According to the 『{{ill|Nihon Ryōiki|ja|日本霊異記}}』, he was captured during the {{ill|Battle of Baekgang|ja|白村江の戦い}} but returned safely thanks to the blessings of {{ill|Kannon|ja|観音菩薩}}, to whom he later dedicated a temple. While the location of this ancient temple remains unknown, it reflects an effort to reassert local authority through Buddhism. In the history of Iyo, the first documented Ochi clan members were Ochi no Atai Asukamaro and Ochi no Atai Kunimaru, who were promoted in 767 ({{ill|Jingō-keiun|ja|神護景雲}} 1) for their tribute offerings. Later, Ochi Mochitada received official recognition in 948 ({{ill|Tenryaku|ja|天暦}} 2) for subduing pirates during the {{ill|Jōhei Tengyō War|ja|承平天慶の乱}}. Despite occasional mentions of the Ochi clan in historical records, the clan disappeared from Iyo’s political scene after Ochi Tamemori’s appointment as a military commissioner in 1002 ({{ill|Chōhō|ja|長保}} 4). == Legacy and Decline == The Ochi clan’s decline is marked by the increasing influence of the {{ill|Kōno clan|ja|河野氏}} in Iyo Province. While sources like 『{{ill|Yoshōki|ja|予章記}}』 provide genealogies linking the Ochi and Kōno clans, these accounts are often embellished with myths, such as tracing the Ochi lineage to Emperor Kōrei’s grandson. Such claims have been met with skepticism due to their lack of historical credibility. According to the {{ill|Ōyamazumi Shrine|ja|大山祇神社}} in modern {{ill|Ōmishima, Imabari|ja|今治市大三島町}}, the Ochi clan became the shrine’s hereditary priests, later adopting the surname {{ill|Mishima|ja|三島家}}. Today, "Ochi" remains a common surname in Imabari City. Notably, during the early Meiji era, documents recorded political leader {{ill|Itō Hirobumi|ja|伊藤博文}} as "Ochi Sukune Hirobumi." == References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist|24em}} == Bibliography == * ''History of Ehime Prefecture – Prefectural History 38'', {{ill|Yamakawa Shuppansha|ja|山川出版社}}, 2003. In November of 835 ({{ill|Jōwa|ja|承和 (日本)}} 2), his descendant Ochi Hiroshige was granted the surname Sukune from Atai (『{{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}}』). {{Sfn|丹羽|1970|p=355}})
Alternatively, Ochiō
({{ill|Emperor Kōrei|ja|孝霊天皇}}’s third prince, {{ill|Prince Iyo|ja|伊予皇子}}’s third son?) | Origin = | Ancestor = | Type = {{ill|Divine lineage|ja|神別}}
Alternatively {{ill|Imperial lineage|ja|皇別}} | Hometown = {{ill|Iyo Province|ja|伊予国}}, {{ill|Ochi-go|ja|越智郷}} | Figures = | Descendants = {{ill|Kōno clan|ja|河野氏}} (Samurai)
{{ill|Yano clan|ja|矢野氏}} (Samurai)
{{ill|Iyo Tachibana clan|ja|橘氏 (伊予国)}} (Samurai)
{{ill|Mishima family|ja|三島家 (薩摩藩士の家系)}} (Samurai)
{{ill|Nii clan|ja|新居氏}} (Samurai)
{{ill|Imai clan|ja|今井氏#その他の今井氏}} (Samurai) }} '''Ochi clan''' (越智氏) refers to a {{ill|clan|ja|氏族}} with the name "Ochi." The Ochi clan was one of the prominent {{ill|Gōzoku|lt=local ruling clans|ja|豪族}} in ancient {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}, originating in {{ill|Iyo Province|ja|伊予国}} (present-day {{ill|Ehime Prefecture|ja|愛媛県}}).{{cite kotobank|word=越智氏|author=山内 譲|encyclopedia=改訂新版 {{ill|World Encyclopedia|ja|世界大百科事典}}|accessdate=2024-03-10}} Some theories link the clan's origins to its association with the development of the {{ill|Nankaidō|ja|南海道}} region, but this connection remains unverified. == Overview == The Ochi clan is said to have originated in {{ill|Ochi-go|ja|越智郷}} (modern {{ill|Kokubu, Imabari|ja|今治市国分}}). During the late 4th century, the establishment of the {{ill|Kuni no miyatsuko|ja|国造}} system by the Yamato court led to the creation of five Kuni no miyatsuko in western Ehime, including the Ochi clan. The clan’s founder is traditionally considered {{ill|Ochinomikoto|ja|小致命}}, grandson of Ōshinkawa no Mikoto of the {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}} lineage (『{{ill|Kuni no miyatsuko no Hongi|ja|国造本紀}}』). According to the 『{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}』, Ochi no Atai (later Ochi Sukune) was a {{ill|Divine lineage|ja|神別}} clan descending from {{ill|Nigihayahi|ja|饒速日命}}.{{cite web|url=https://www.digital.archives.go.jp/file/1256162.html|title=Shinsen Shōjiroku|website=National Archives of Japan|accessdate=2024-03-10}} During the early 8th century, the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} reorganized the administrative divisions into fourteen districts, designating the former Ochi Kuni no Miyatsuko territory as "Ochi District." The district became the site of Iyo’s {{ill|provincial government|ja|国府}} and {{ill|provincial temple|ja|国分寺}}. Archaeological investigations near Kokubu have suggested a connection between the Ochi clan and the provincial government, but conclusive evidence is lacking. The earliest recorded figure of the Ochi clan is Ochi no Atai, a district governor of Ochi District. According to the 『{{ill|Nihon Ryōiki|ja|日本霊異記}}』, he was captured during the {{ill|Battle of Baekgang|ja|白村江の戦い}} but returned safely thanks to the blessings of {{ill|Kannon|ja|観音菩薩}}, to whom he later dedicated a temple. While the location of this ancient temple remains unknown, it reflects an effort to reassert local authority through Buddhism. In the history of Iyo, the first documented Ochi clan members were Ochi no Atai Asukamaro and Ochi no Atai Kunimaru, who were promoted in 767 ({{ill|Jingō-keiun|ja|神護景雲}} 1) for their tribute offerings. Later, Ochi Mochitada received official recognition in 948 ({{ill|Tenryaku|ja|天暦}} 2) for subduing pirates during the {{ill|Jōhei Tengyō War|ja|承平天慶の乱}}. Despite occasional mentions of the Ochi clan in historical records, the clan disappeared from Iyo’s political scene after Ochi Tamemori’s appointment as a military commissioner in 1002 ({{ill|Chōhō|ja|長保}} 4). == Legacy and Decline == The Ochi clan’s decline is marked by the increasing influence of the {{ill|Kōno clan|ja|河野氏}} in Iyo Province. While sources like 『{{ill|Yoshōki|ja|予章記}}』 provide genealogies linking the Ochi and Kōno clans, these accounts are often embellished with myths, such as tracing the Ochi lineage to Emperor Kōrei’s grandson. Such claims have been met with skepticism due to their lack of historical credibility. According to the {{ill|Ōyamazumi Shrine|ja|大山祇神社}} in modern {{ill|Ōmishima, Imabari|ja|今治市大三島町}}, the Ochi clan became the shrine’s hereditary priests, later adopting the surname {{ill|Mishima|ja|三島家}}. Today, "Ochi" remains a common surname in Imabari City. Notably, during the early Meiji era, documents recorded political leader {{ill|Itō Hirobumi|ja|伊藤博文}} as "Ochi Sukune Hirobumi." == References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist|24em}} == Bibliography == * ''History of Ehime Prefecture – Prefectural History 38'', {{ill|Yamakawa Shuppansha|ja|山川出版社}}, 2003. {{pp-vandalism|small=yes}} {{otheruses|the Ochi clan of Iyo Province|a branch of the Yamato Minamoto clan|Ochi clan (Yamato Province)}} {{Japanese clans (ancient clans) | Name = Ochi clan | image = | Clan name = Ochi [[Sukune]] | Founder = {{ill|Ochinomikoto|ja|小致命}}
({{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}} lineage, {{ill|Ochi Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|小市国造}}The Ochi clan descends from Mononobe’s Ochinomikoto, who was appointed as the Ochi Kuni no Miyatsuko (Governor of Ochi County) and controlled Ochi District under the title "Atai." In November of 835 ({{ill|Jōwa|ja|承和 (日本)}} 2), his descendant Ochi Hiroshige was granted the surname Sukune from Atai (『{{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}}』). {{Sfn|丹羽|1970|p=355}})
Alternatively, Ochiō
({{ill|Emperor Kōrei|ja|孝霊天皇}}’s third prince, {{ill|Prince Iyo|ja|伊予皇子}}’s third son?) | Origin = | Ancestor = | Type = {{ill|Divine lineage|ja|神別}}
Alternatively {{ill|Imperial lineage|ja|皇別}} | Hometown = {{ill|Iyo Province|ja|伊予国}}, {{ill|Ochi-go|ja|越智郷}} | Figures = | Descendants = {{ill|Kōno clan|ja|河野氏}} (Samurai)
{{ill|Yano clan|ja|矢野氏}} (Samurai)
{{ill|Iyo Tachibana clan|ja|橘氏 (伊予国)}} (Samurai)
{{ill|Mishima family|ja|三島家 (薩摩藩士の家系)}} (Samurai)
{{ill|Nii clan|ja|新居氏}} (Samurai)
{{ill|Imai clan|ja|今井氏#その他の今井氏}} (Samurai) }} '''Ochi clan''' (越智氏) refers to a {{ill|clan|ja|氏族}} with the name "Ochi." The Ochi clan was one of the prominent {{ill|Gōzoku|lt=local ruling clans|ja|豪族}} in ancient {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}, originating in {{ill|Iyo Province|ja|伊予国}} (present-day {{ill|Ehime Prefecture|ja|愛媛県}}).{{cite kotobank|word=越智氏|author=山内 譲|encyclopedia=改訂新版 {{ill|World Encyclopedia|ja|世界大百科事典}}|accessdate=2024-03-10}} Some theories link the clan's origins to its association with the development of the {{ill|Nankaidō|ja|南海道}} region, but this connection remains unverified. == Overview == The Ochi clan is said to have originated in {{ill|Ochi-go|ja|越智郷}} (modern {{ill|Kokubu, Imabari|ja|今治市国分}}). During the late 4th century, the establishment of the {{ill|Kuni no miyatsuko|ja|国造}} system by the Yamato court led to the creation of five Kuni no miyatsuko in western Ehime, including the Ochi clan. The clan’s founder is traditionally considered {{ill|Ochinomikoto|ja|小致命}}, grandson of Ōshinkawa no Mikoto of the {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}} lineage (『{{ill|Kuni no miyatsuko no Hongi|ja|国造本紀}}』). According to the 『{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}』, Ochi no Atai (later Ochi Sukune) was a {{ill|Divine lineage|ja|神別}} clan descending from {{ill|Nigihayahi|ja|饒速日命}}.{{cite web|url=https://www.digital.archives.go.jp/file/1256162.html|title=Shinsen Shōjiroku|website=National Archives of Japan|accessdate=2024-03-10}} During the early 8th century, the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} reorganized the administrative divisions into fourteen districts, designating the former Ochi Kuni no Miyatsuko territory as "Ochi District." The district became the site of Iyo’s {{ill|provincial government|ja|国府}} and {{ill|provincial temple|ja|国分寺}}. Archaeological investigations near Kokubu have suggested a connection between the Ochi clan and the provincial government, but conclusive evidence is lacking. The earliest recorded figure of the Ochi clan is Ochi no Atai, a district governor of Ochi District. According to the 『{{ill|Nihon Ryōiki|ja|日本霊異記}}』, he was captured during the {{ill|Battle of Baekgang|ja|白村江の戦い}} but returned safely thanks to the blessings of {{ill|Kannon|ja|観音菩薩}}, to whom he later dedicated a temple. While the location of this ancient temple remains unknown, it reflects an effort to reassert local authority through Buddhism. In the history of Iyo, the first documented Ochi clan members were Ochi no Atai Asukamaro and Ochi no Atai Kunimaru, who were promoted in 767 ({{ill|Jingō-keiun|ja|神護景雲}} 1) for their tribute offerings. Later, Ochi Mochitada received official recognition in 948 ({{ill|Tenryaku|ja|天暦}} 2) for subduing pirates during the {{ill|Jōhei Tengyō War|ja|承平天慶の乱}}. Despite occasional mentions of the Ochi clan in historical records, the clan disappeared from Iyo’s political scene after Ochi Tamemori’s appointment as a military commissioner in 1002 ({{ill|Chōhō|ja|長保}} 4). == Legacy and Decline == The Ochi clan’s decline is marked by the increasing influence of the {{ill|Kōno clan|ja|河野氏}} in Iyo Province. While sources like 『{{ill|Yoshōki|ja|予章記}}』 provide genealogies linking the Ochi and Kōno clans, these accounts are often embellished with myths, such as tracing the Ochi lineage to Emperor Kōrei’s grandson. Such claims have been met with skepticism due to their lack of historical credibility. According to the {{ill|Ōyamazumi Shrine|ja|大山祇神社}} in modern {{ill|Ōmishima, Imabari|ja|今治市大三島町}}, the Ochi clan became the shrine’s hereditary priests, later adopting the surname {{ill|Mishima|ja|三島家}}. Today, "Ochi" remains a common surname in Imabari City. Notably, during the early Meiji era, documents recorded political leader {{ill|Itō Hirobumi|ja|伊藤博文}} as "Ochi Sukune Hirobumi." == References == {{Reflist|24em}} == Bibliography == * ''History of Ehime Prefecture – Prefectural History 38'', {{ill|Yamakawa Shuppansha|ja|山川出版社}}, 2003. ISBN 4-634-32380-X * {{Citation|和書|last=丹羽|first=基二|author-link=丹羽基二|others=[[樋口清之]]監修|date=1970-7|title=Surnames: The Definitive Study of Japanese Family Names|publisher=[[Akita Shoten]]|isbn=4253002099|ref=harv}} == Related Articles == {{div col||10em}} * {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}} * {{ill|Takaichi clan|ja|高市氏}} * {{ill|Nii clan|ja|新居氏}} * {{ill|Bekku clan|ja|別宮氏}} * {{ill|Kōno clan|ja|河野氏}} * {{ill|Iyo Tachibana clan|ja|橘氏 (伊予国)}} * {{ill|Yoshōki|ja|予章記}} * {{ill|Ochi clan (Yamato Province)|ja|越智氏 (Yamato Province )}} {{div col end}} == External Links == * {{Wayback|url=http://www17.ocn.ne.jp/~kanada/1234-7-15.html |title=Study on the Ochi Clan |date=20080105084406}} * [http://www2.harimaya.com/sengoku/html/oti_k.html Bushi Genealogies — Ochi Clan] {{pp-vandalism|small=yes}} {{otheruses|the Ochi clan of Iyo Province|a branch of the Yamato Minamoto clan|Ochi clan (Yamato Province)}} {{Japanese clans (ancient clans) | Name = Ochi clan | image = | Clan name = Ochi [[Sukune]] | Founder = {{ill|Ochinomikoto|ja|小致命}}
({{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}} lineage, {{ill|Ochi Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|小市国造}}The Ochi clan descends from Mononobe’s Ochinomikoto, who was appointed as the Ochi Kuni no Miyatsuko (Governor of Ochi County) and controlled Ochi District under the title "Atai." In November of 835 ({{ill|Jōwa|ja|承和 (日本)}} 2), his descendant Ochi Hiroshige was granted the surname Sukune from Atai (『{{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}}』). {{Sfn|丹羽|1970|p=355}})
Alternatively, Ochiō
({{ill|Emperor Kōrei|ja|孝霊天皇}}’s third prince, {{ill|Prince Iyo|ja|伊予皇子}}’s third son?) | Origin = | Ancestor = | Type = {{ill|Divine lineage|ja|神別}}
Alternatively {{ill|Imperial lineage|ja|皇別}} | Hometown = {{ill|Iyo Province|ja|伊予国}}, {{ill|Ochi-go|ja|越智郷}} | Figures = | Descendants = {{ill|Kōno clan|ja|河野氏}} (Samurai)
{{ill|Yano clan|ja|矢野氏}} (Samurai)
{{ill|Iyo Tachibana clan|ja|橘氏 (伊予国)}} (Samurai)
{{ill|Mishima family|ja|三島家 (薩摩藩士の家系)}} (Samurai)
{{ill|Nii clan|ja|新居氏}} (Samurai)
{{ill|Imai clan|ja|今井氏#その他の今井氏}} (Samurai) }} '''Ochi clan''' (越智氏) refers to a {{ill|clan|ja|氏族}} with the name "Ochi." The Ochi clan was one of the prominent {{ill|Gōzoku|lt=local ruling clans|ja|豪族}} in ancient {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}, originating in {{ill|Iyo Province|ja|伊予国}} (present-day {{ill|Ehime Prefecture|ja|愛媛県}}).{{cite kotobank|word=越智氏|author=山内 譲|encyclopedia=改訂新版 {{ill|World Encyclopedia|ja|世界大百科事典}}|accessdate=2024-03-10}} Some theories link the clan's origins to its association with the development of the {{ill|Nankaidō|ja|南海道}} region, but this connection remains unverified. == Overview == The Ochi clan is said to have originated in {{ill|Ochi-go|ja|越智郷}} (modern {{ill|Kokubu, Imabari|ja|今治市国分}}). During the late 4th century, the establishment of the {{ill|Kuni no miyatsuko|ja|国造}} system by the Yamato court led to the creation of five Kuni no miyatsuko in western Ehime, including the Ochi clan. The clan’s founder is traditionally considered {{ill|Ochinomikoto|ja|小致命}}, grandson of Ōshinkawa no Mikoto of the {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}} lineage (『{{ill|Kuni no miyatsuko no Hongi|ja|国造本紀}}』). According to the 『{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|ja|新撰姓氏録|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}}』, Ochi no Atai (later Ochi Sukune) was a {{ill|Divine lineage|ja|神別}} clan descending from {{ill|Nigihayahi|ja|饒速日命}}.{{cite web|url=https://www.digital.archives.go.jp/file/1256162.html|title=Shinsen Shōjiroku|website=National Archives of Japan|accessdate=2024-03-10}} During the early 8th century, the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} reorganized the administrative divisions into fourteen districts, designating the former Ochi Kuni no Miyatsuko territory as "Ochi District." The district became the site of Iyo’s {{ill|provincial government|ja|国府}} and {{ill|provincial temple|ja|国分寺}}. Archaeological investigations near Kokubu have suggested a connection between the Ochi clan and the provincial government, but conclusive evidence is lacking. The earliest recorded figure of the Ochi clan is Ochi no Atai, a district governor of Ochi District. According to the 『{{ill|Nihon Ryōiki|ja|日本霊異記}}』, he was captured during the {{ill|Battle of Baekgang|ja|白村江の戦い}} but returned safely thanks to the blessings of {{ill|Kannon|ja|観音菩薩}}, to whom he later dedicated a temple. While the location of this ancient temple remains unknown, it reflects an effort to reassert local authority through Buddhism. In the history of Iyo, the first documented Ochi clan members were Ochi no Atai Asukamaro and Ochi no Atai Kunimaru, who were promoted in 767 ({{ill|Jingō-keiun|ja|神護景雲}} 1) for their tribute offerings. Later, Ochi Mochitada received official recognition in 948 ({{ill|Tenryaku|ja|天暦}} 2) for subduing pirates during the {{ill|Jōhei Tengyō War|ja|承平天慶の乱}}. Despite occasional mentions of the Ochi clan in historical records, the clan disappeared from Iyo’s political scene after Ochi Tamemori’s appointment as a military commissioner in 1002 ({{ill|Chōhō|ja|長保}} 4). == Legacy and Decline == The Ochi clan’s decline is marked by the increasing influence of the {{ill|Kōno clan|ja|河野氏}} in Iyo Province. While sources like 『{{ill|Yoshōki|ja|予章記}}』 provide genealogies linking the Ochi and Kōno clans, these accounts are often embellished with myths, such as tracing the Ochi lineage to Emperor Kōrei’s grandson. Such claims have been met with skepticism due to their lack of historical credibility. According to the {{ill|Ōyamazumi Shrine|ja|大山祇神社|1=Ōyamazumi Shrine}} in modern {{ill|Ōmishima, Imabari|ja|今治市大三島町}}, the Ochi clan became the shrine’s hereditary priests, later adopting the surname {{ill|Mishima|ja|三島家}}. Today, "Ochi" remains a common surname in Imabari City. Notably, during the early Meiji era, documents recorded political leader {{ill|Itō Hirobumi|ja|伊藤博文}} as "Ochi Sukune Hirobumi." == References == {{Reflist|24em}} == Bibliography == * ''History of Ehime Prefecture – Prefectural History 38'', {{ill|Yamakawa Shuppansha|ja|山川出版社}}, 2003. ISBN 4-634-32380-X * {{Citation|和書|last=丹羽|first=基二|author-link=丹羽基二|others=[[樋口清之]]監修|date=1970-7|title=Surnames: The Definitive Study of Japanese Family Names|publisher=[[Akita Shoten]]|isbn=4253002099|ref=harv}} == Related Articles == {{div col||10em}} * {{ill|Mononobe clan|ja|物部氏}} * {{ill|Takaichi clan|ja|高市氏}} * {{ill|Nii clan|ja|新居氏}} * {{ill|Bekku clan|ja|別宮氏}} * {{ill|Kōno clan|ja|河野氏}} * {{ill|Iyo Tachibana clan|ja|橘氏 (伊予国)}} * {{ill|Yoshōki|ja|予章記}} * {{ill|Ochi clan (Yamato Province)|ja|越智氏 (Yamato Province )}} {{div col end}} == External Links == * {{Wayback|url=http://www17.ocn.ne.jp/~kanada/1234-7-15.html |title=Study on the Ochi Clan |date=20080105084406}} * [http://www2.harimaya.com/sengoku/html/oti_k.html Bushi Genealogies — Ochi Clan] revision.text:
{{otheruses|伊予国の越智氏|[[大和源氏]]の一門|越智氏 (大和国)}} {{日本の氏族 (古代氏族) |氏名 = 越智氏 |画像 = |氏姓 = 越智[[宿禰]] |始祖 = [[小致命]]
（[[物部氏|物部姓]][[小市国造]]物部の一族である小致命（子到命＝おちのみこと）が小市国造（おちのくにのみやつこ）に任命されて、越智郡を支配して直（あたえ）姓を称した。[[835年]]（[[承和 (日本)|承和]]2年）11月に、その子孫の越智広成が直から宿禰姓を賜ったと述べている（『[[先代旧事本紀|旧事本紀]]』）。{{Sfn|丹羽|1970|p=355}}）
一説に小千王
（[[孝霊天皇]]の第3[[皇子]][[伊予皇子]]の第3[[王子]]？） |出自 = |氏祖 = |種別 = [[神別]]
一説に[[皇別]] |本貫 = [[伊予国]][[越智郷]] |人物 = |後裔 = [[河野氏]]（[[武家]]）
[[矢野氏]]（武家）
[[橘氏 (伊予国)|伊予橘氏]]（武家）
[[三島家 (薩摩藩士の家系)|三島氏]]（武家）
[[新居氏]]（武家）
[[今井氏#その他の今井氏|伊予今井氏]]（武家） }} '''越智氏'''（おちうじ）は、「越智」を[[氏]]の名とする[[氏族]]。 [[古代]][[日本]]の[[伊予国]]（[[愛媛県]]）の[[豪族]]の一つ{{cite kotobank|word=越智氏|author=山内 譲|encyclopedia=改訂新版 [[世界大百科事典]]|accessdate=2024-03-10}}。その由来は伊予守輩出や、[[南海道]]の発展から伊予越智氏と関係があったとする説もあるが、立証はなく関係性があったかは不明である。 == 概要 == 越智氏は越智郷（現在の[[今治市]]国分付近）が出自とされる{{r|kot}}。[[4世紀]]後半に近畿政権の[[国造|国造制]]により、現在の愛媛西部に五国造が設置され、中央豪族が地域の支配者に任じられた。そのうちひとつである[[物部氏]]の大新河命の孫の'''小致命'''（おちのみこと）が'''[[小市国造]]'''である越智氏の始まりとされている（『[[国造本紀]]』）。『[[新撰姓氏録]]』では左京の[[神別]]氏族として越智直が[[神饒速日命]]の後と見える{{cite web|和書|url=https://www.digital.archives.go.jp/file/1256162.html|title=新撰姓氏録|website=国立公文書館デジタルアーカイブ|accessdate=2024-03-10}}。 [[8世紀]]初頭には、[[律令制]]により十四郡に増え小市国造が「[[越智郡]]」となり、そこへ伊予の[[国府]]や[[国分寺]]が置かれた。『[[和名類聚抄]]』　越智氏と国府の関係において、越智氏が元々在地豪族だったとする説もある。律令制国府の場所については国分寺近辺に見当をつけ[[発掘調査]]も一部されたが確定には至らなかった。 越智氏人物として初めて史上に登場するのは、越智郡大領の先祖である越智[[直 (姓)|直]]（おちのあたい）である。[[白村江の戦い]]で捕虜になったが、[[観音菩薩]]の霊験により無事帰還することができ、観音菩薩を奉じて寺を建てたという話が[[日本現報善悪霊異記|日本霊異記]]に載せられている。支配力が低下する中、[[仏教]]によって在地支配を再構築しようとした様子が伺われる（建立された古代寺院跡の場所は不明。）。 伊予史上では、[[神護景雲]]元年（[[767年]]）に献物により叙位された越智郡大領の越智直飛鳥麻呂と越智直国益が最初である。[[天暦]]2年（[[948年]]）には[[伊予国]]から海賊平定の功により[[越智用忠]]への叙位が認められており[[承平天慶の乱]]で政府軍側に立って戦ったことがわかる。[[永延]]元年（[[987年]]）[[東三条第]]で[[相撲]]があり、[[越智常世]]が伊予より助手として参加するなど、その後も史上には登場するものの、越智為保を伊予追捕使に任命[[長保]]4年（[[1002年]]）以降、越智氏が伊予の表舞台に登場することはない。 [[寛仁]]2年（[[1018年]]）に、[[伊予国|伊予守]][[源頼光]]が[[藤原道長]]に[[土御門殿]]の家具一切を献上したとあり、11.[[12世紀]]から国営支配の実権を握っていたのは[[在庁官人]]と呼ばれた地方豪族だったことからも、[[源氏]]は伊予守時代に越智氏と何らかの関係性が考えられ、源頼光が[[清和源氏]]三代目で、異母弟に[[源頼親]]が居ることや、越智氏のその後が表立って伊予で確認出来ない時期的なことからも、活躍の場を大和に移したとしても不思議ではない。 [[河野氏]]の記した『[[予章記]]』などで語られることの多い越智氏だが、[[孝霊天皇]]の第三[[皇子]][[彦狭島命|伊予皇子]]の第三[[王 (皇族)|王子]]を祖とするなどの粉飾が加えられていたりと信憑性に乏しく、越智氏自体の歴史は分からないことが多い。一般に越智氏系譜は河野氏系譜と混同視されており、疑問を投げかける声は少なくない。 [[今治市]][[大三島町]]の[[大山祇神社]]によれば河野氏を分家した後も代々ここの神職（大祝職）を担い、鎌倉時代には家名を大祝氏と名を変え現在これを担う[[三島家 (大山祇神社社家)|三島家]]へと姿を変えたとされている。また、越智の姓は現在でも今治市で最も多い姓として現代に残されている。なお、カバネの制度が残っていた明治初期の公文書では、[[伊藤博文]]が「越智宿禰博文」と姓（カバネ）と諱（いみな）を記載している。 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist|24em}} == 参考文献 == * 愛媛県の歴史―県史38（[[山川出版社]]、[[2003年]]） ISBN 4-634-32380-X * {{Citation|和書|last=丹羽|first=基二|author-link=丹羽基二|others=[[樋口清之]]監修|date=1970-7|title=姓氏 : 姓氏研究の決定版|publisher=[[秋田書店]]|isbn=4253002099|ref=harv}} == 関連項目 == {{div col||10em}} * [[物部氏]] * [[高市氏]] * [[新居氏]] * 別宮氏 * [[河野氏]] * [[橘氏 (伊予国)]] * [[予章記]] * [[越智氏 (大和国)]] {{div col end}} == 外部リンク == * {{Wayback|url=http://www17.ocn.ne.jp/~kanada/1234-7-15.html |title=越智氏考 |date=20080105084406}} * [http://www2.harimaya.com/sengoku/html/oti_k.html 武家家伝{{mdash}}越智氏] {{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:おちうし}} [[Category:越智氏|!]] [[Category:氏]] [[category:日本の氏族]] [[Category:今治市の歴史]] [[Category:神別]]
